sections.heading: This Act is the Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2020 and comes into
sections.title: Excluded circumstances for porting data transmission
sections.summary: A porting organisation is exempt from transmitting applicable data under section 26H(2) in circumstances where data transmission would unreasonably interfere with operations due to repetitive requests, the burden or expense is unreasonable, the data does not exist or is trivial, or the request is frivolous or vexatious. These exclusions apply to the organisation's obligations regarding data porting requests.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines specific circumstances under which data transmission is not required, which is critical for DTIA assessments to determine when data porting obligations are waived without affecting cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: A porting organisation is not required to transmit any applicable data under section 26H(2) in any of the following circumstances
transmitting the applicable data will unreasonably interfere with the operations of the porting organisation because of the repetitious or systematic nature of the data porting request
the burden or expense of transmitting the applicable data is unreasonable to the porting organisation or disproportionate to the individual's interests
the data porting request relates to applicable data that — (i) does not exist or cannot be found; or (ii) is trivial
the data porting request is frivolous or vexatious

sections.source_quote: A porting organisation is not required to transmit any applicable data under section 26H(2) in any of the following circumstances: (a) transmitting the applicable data will unreasonably interfere with the operations of the porting organisation because of the repetitious or systematic nature of the data porting request; (b) the burden or expense of transmitting the applicable data is unreasonable to the porting organisation or disproportionate to the individual's interests; (c) the data porting request relates to applicable data that — (i) does not exist or cannot be found; or (ii) is trivial; (d) the data porting request is frivolous or vexatious.
sections.relevance_reason: Excludes data porting under specific conditions

sections.title: Consideration of data porting request frequency
sections.summary: For determining whether data porting requests are excessive, an organisation may consider the number and frequency of such requests received for the applicable data. This provision addresses the assessment of request volume when evaluating exemptions under data porting mechanisms.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies that the frequency of data porting requests can be a factor in determining exemptions, which is relevant for DTIA assessments when evaluating the proportionality of data transmission obligations.

sections.title: Assessing anonymised information systems
sections.summary: This section mandates that organizations or public agencies assess, test, or evaluate their systems and processes to ensure the integrity and confidentiality of anonymised information. It covers both data in their possession and information transmitted or received by the organization.

sections.title: Disclosure of personal data to law enforcement officers
sections.summary: This section permits disclosure of personal data to prescribed law enforcement officers when they provide written authorisation signed by their head or equivalent officer, certifying the data is necessary for their functions. The disclosure must be for law enforcement purposes and requires specific documentation to validate necessity.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a legal mechanism for cross-border data transfers to law enforcement agencies with specific conditions on necessity and authorisation, relevant for DTIAs involving international law enforcement cooperation.

sections.title: Data collection for legal services or credit reports
sections.summary: This section permits data collection for legal services or credit reporting purposes. It allows organisations to collect personal data when providing legal services to individuals or when credit bureaus prepare or provide credit reports for transactions between members and individuals.

requirements.text: The collection, use or disclosure of personal data about an individual is necessary for the provision of legal services by the organisation to another person, or for the organisation to obtain legal services

sections.source_quote: 6. In this Part — "broadcasting service" has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Broadcasting Act (Cap. 28); "news activity" means — (a) the gathering of news, or the preparation or compilation of articles or programmes of or concerning news, observations on news, or current affairs, for the purposes of dissemination to the public or any section of the public; or (b) the dissemination, to the public or any section of the public, of any article or programme of or concerning — (i) news; (ii) observations on news; or (iii) current affairs; "news organisation" means — (a) any organisation — (i) the business of which consists, in whole or in part, of news activity carried out in relation to a relevant broadcasting service, a newswire service or the publication of a newspaper; (g) special interest television services; (h) free-to-air nationwide radio services; (i) free-to-air localised radio services; (j) free-to-air international radio services; (k) subscription nationwide radio services; sections.summary: This section defines personal data provided by an individual to an organisation for the individual's personal or domestic purposes. sections.title: Amendment of financial penalty limits
sections.summary: This amendment sets maximum financial penalties for organizations and individuals based on turnover thresholds. Organizations with Singapore turnover exceeding $10 million face penalties up to 10% of turnover, while others face $1 million. Individuals face $200,000 or 5% of turnover (for large entities), with a $1 million cap.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines maximum financial penalties for data breaches, directly impacting the financial risk assessment for data transfers and processing activities.

Financial penalty for individuals must not exceed $200,000

sections.source_quote: A financial penalty imposed on an organisation under subsection (1)(a) must not exceed the maximum amount to be prescribed, which in no case may be more than the following: (a) in the case of a contravention on or after the date of commencement of section 24 of the Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2020 by an organisation whose annual turnover in Singapore exceeds $10 million — 10% of the annual turnover in Singapore of the organisation;

sections.heading: The principal Act is amended by inserting, immediately after section 26, the
sections.title: Amendment to insert Part VIA for data breach notifications
sections.summary: This section amends the Act by inserting Part VIA, which establishes requirements for organisations to assess and notify data breaches to the Personal Data Protection Commission. It mandates that organisations conduct assessments to determine if a breach constitutes a notifiable data breach, and requires timely notification to the Commission and affected individuals where applicable. The rules also address data intermediary responsibilities and exceptions for certain breaches.
sections.dtia_summary: This section creates specific obligations for organisations to assess and report data breaches, which directly impacts data transfer and storage practices across borders by requiring breach notifications and assessments for cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: Organisations must conduct a reasonable and expeditious assessment to determine if a data breach is a notifiable data breach
Organisations must notify the Commission within 3 calendar days of assessing a notifiable data breach

Data intermediaries must notify other organisations of breaches they detect
Organisations may exclude notification if actions taken render breach unlikely to cause harm
Organisations may exclude notification if instructed by a law enforcement agency

sections.source_quote: A data breach is a notifiable data breach if the data breach — (a) results in, or is likely to result in, significant harm to an affected individual; or (b) is, or is likely to be, of a significant scale.
sections.relevance_reason: Breach notification core requirement

sections.title: Definitions for data protection sections
sections.summary: This section provides definitions for key terms used in sections 14A, 14B, and 14C, including 'gain', 'generally available information', 'harm', 'loss', 'personal data', and 'prescribed circumstances' or 'prescribed purpose'. harm, in relation to an individual, means — (a) any physical harm; or (b) harassment, alarm or distress caused to the individual

loss means — (a) a loss in property or a supply of services, whether temporary or permanent; or (b) a loss of an opportunity to earn remuneration or greater remuneration or to gain a financial advantage otherwise than by way of remuneration, but excludes, in relation to an individual, the loss of personal data about the individual
personal data has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (Act 26 of 2012)

sections.source_quote: In sections 14A, 14B and 14C — gain means — (a) a gain in property or a supply of services, whether temporary or permanent; or (b) an opportunity to earn remuneration or greater remuneration or to gain a financial advantage otherwise than by way of remuneration; generally available information means information that consists of readily observable matter, including information that consists of deductions, conclusions or inferences made or drawn from readily observable matter; harm, in relation to an individual, means — (a) any physical harm; or (b) harassment, alarm or distress caused to the individual; loss means — (a) a loss in property or a supply of services, whether temporary or permanent; or (b) a loss of an opportunity to earn remuneration or greater remuneration or to gain a financial advantage otherwise than by way of remuneration, but excludes, in relation to an individual, the loss of personal data about the individual; personal data has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (Act 26 of 2012); prescribed circumstances or prescribed purpose means any circumstances or purpose prescribed in regulations made under section 14D.

sections.heading: Section 43 of the principal Act is repealed and the following section substituted
sections.title: Verification of Singapore phone numbers before sending messages
sections.summary: A person must not send a message to a Singapore telephone number without valid confirmation that the number is not listed in the relevant register. Valid confirmation requires either a Commission application with confirmation that the number is not listed, or obtaining information from a checker that is accurate and not expired or false. The consent of the recipient must be clear and unambiguous and evidenced in writing for the message to be exempt from this rule.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires verification of Singapore phone numbers before cross-border messaging to prevent unauthorized use of numbers listed in registers. This affects data transfer mechanisms involving international SMS or calls.

sections.heading: The principal Act is amended by inserting, immediately after section 48, the (cont.)
sections.title: Unauthorised use of personal data: criminal penalties and defences
sections.summary: This section defines criminal offences for individuals who improperly use personal data without authorization. It specifies that an offence occurs when an individual uses personal data in the possession or control of an organisation or public agency without authorization, knowing or being reckless about the lack of authorization, and causes a gain, harm, or loss. The maximum penalty is a fine of $5,000 or imprisonment for two years. The defence allows proving the data was publicly available or the use was authorized by law, court order, or reasonable belief in legal right.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes criminal liability for unauthorized use of personal data, which impacts DTIAs by requiring assessments of data usage permissions and potential legal defences to avoid criminal penalties.

requirements.text: If an individual uses personal data without authorization by an organisation or public agency
The use must be known or reckless regarding lack of authorization
The use must cause a gain for the individual or another person
The use must cause harm to another individual
The use must cause a loss to another person
The individual is liable to a fine of up to $5,000 or imprisonment for two years
The accused may prove the data was publicly available as a defence
The accused may prove the use was authorized by law, court order, or reasonable belief in legal right as a defence

sections.source_quote: If — (a) an individual makes use of personal data in the possession or under the control of an organisation or a public agency; (b) the use is not authorised by the organisation or public agency, as the case may be; (c) the individual does so — (i) knowing that the use is not authorised by the organisation or public agency, as the case may be; or (ii) reckless as to whether the use is or is not authorised by the organisation or public agency, as the case may be; and (d) the individual, as a result of that use — (i) obtains a gain for the individual or another person; (ii) causes harm to another individual; or (iii) causes a loss to another person, the individual shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding $5,000 or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 2 years or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Criminal penalties for unauthorized data use

sections.heading: The principal Act is amended by inserting, immediately before Part X, the
sections.title: Enforcement mechanisms for data protection complaints
sections.summary: This section outlines enforcement powers of the Commission for data protection complaints. It allows the Commission to refer complaints to mediation, direct resolution attempts, establish dispute resolution schemes, review refusals to provide data access or corrections, and impose financial penalties on non-compliance. The Commission may also direct organisations to stop data collection, destroy data, or comply with other directions. Financial penalties for organisations cap at $1 million and for individuals at $200,000.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides enforcement mechanisms that impact DTIAs by requiring assessments of compliance with data handling practices and potential penalties for non-compliance, particularly for financial institutions.

requirements.text: The Commission may refer complaints to mediation without consent
The Commission may direct resolution attempts with or without consent
The Commission may establish dispute resolution schemes
The Commission may make regulations for dispute resolution schemes
The Commission may review refusals to provide data access or corrections
The Commission may review refusals to transmit data during porting
The Commission may direct organisations to stop collecting, using or disclosing personal data
The Commission may direct organisations to destroy personal data
The Commission may impose financial penalties on organisations up to $1 million
The Commission may impose financial penalties on individuals up to $200,000

sections.source_quote: The Commission may, if it is satisfied that an organisation has not complied or is not complying with any provision of Part III, IV, V, VI, VIA or VIB, give the organisation any direction that the Commission thinks fit in the circumstances to ensure compliance with that provision.
sections.relevance_reason: Enforcement powers for data protection complaints

sections.heading: The principal Act is amended by inserting, immediately before Part X, the (cont.)
sections.title: Commission notice requirements for enforcement actions
sections.summary: The Commission must serve specific notices to organizations and complainants following financial penalties or directions. Notices must specify payment deadlines for penalties, allow extensions or installment payments, and calculate interest rates not exceeding court judgment debt rates. The Commission may also accept voluntary undertakings to address compliance issues.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes notice requirements for enforcement actions, including payment deadlines, extensions, and interest calculations that impact data transfer compliance timelines and financial penalties.

requirements.text: Commission must serve notice on organization concerned
Commission must serve notice on complainant who resulted in penalty
Financial penalty takes effect only after notice served on organization
Written notice must specify payment deadline not earlier than 28 days
Commission may extend time for penalty payment
Commission may allow penalty payment by instalments
Interest rate must not exceed court judgment debt rate

sections.source_quote: Commission must serve a notice of the decision on the following persons: (a) the organisation or person concerned; (b) the complainant whose complaint against the organisation or person concerned resulted in the giving of the direction or the imposition of the financial penalty (as the case may be), if any.
sections.relevance_reason: enforcement notices for penalties

sections.heading: The principal Act is amended by inserting, immediately before Part X, the (cont.)
sections.title: Data Protection Appeal Panel establishment
sections.summary: The Act establishes a Data Protection Appeal Panel with a Chairman appointed by the Minister. The Panel may form committees to hear appeals against Commission decisions. The Seventh Schedule governs the Panel's procedures and members.
sections.dtia_summary: This section creates an appeal mechanism for organizations and individuals to challenge Commission decisions, affecting data transfer compliance through formal review processes.

requirements.text: Data Protection Appeal Panel is established
Minister appoints Appeal Panel members
Chairman of Appeal Panel appointed by Minister from members
Chairman may nominate Data Protection Appeal Committee

sections.heading: Section 48J of the principal Act, as inserted by section 23, is amended —
sections.title: Amendment of financial penalty limits
sections.summary: This amendment sets maximum financial penalties for organizations and individuals based on turnover thresholds. Organizations with Singapore turnover exceeding $10 million face penalties up to 10% of turnover, while others face $1 million. Individuals face $200,000 or 5% of turnover (for large entities), with a $1 million cap.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines maximum financial penalties for data breaches, directly impacting the financial risk assessment for data transfers and processing activities.

requirements.text: Financial penalty for organizations must not exceed 10% of Singapore turnover (for entities >$10m turnover)
Financial penalty for organizations must not exceed $1 million (for other cases)
Financial penalty for individuals must not exceed $200,000
Financial penalty for individuals must not exceed 5% of Singapore turnover (for entities >$20m turnover)
Financial penalty for individuals must not exceed $1 million (for other cases)

sections.source_quote: A financial penalty imposed on an organisation under subsection (1)(a) must not exceed the maximum amount to be prescribed, which in no case may be more than the following: (a) in the case of a contravention on or after the date of commencement of section 24 of the Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2020 by an organisation whose annual turnover in Singapore exceeds $10 million — 10% of the annual turnover in Singapore of the organisation;
sections.relevance_reason: Penalties for data breaches

sections.heading: Section 48R of the principal Act, as inserted by section 23, is amended —
sections.title: Amendment of court jurisdiction for appeals
sections.summary: This amendment changes court jurisdiction for appeals from the Data Protection Appeal Committee to the General Division of the High Court, removing references to 'High Court' in relevant sections. The amendment also deletes subsection (5) of section 48R.
sections.dtia_summary: This section modifies the judicial pathway for appeals, affecting the enforcement mechanisms for data transfer compliance decisions.

requirements.text: Appeals from Appeal Committee to General Division of High Court

sections.source_quote: Section 48R of the principal Act, as inserted by section 23, is amended — (a) by deleting the words 'High Court' wherever they appear in subsections (1), (3) and (4) and substituting in each case the words 'General Division of the High Court';
sections.relevance_reason: Changes court jurisdiction, no data impact

sections.heading: Section 50 of the principal Act is amended —
sections.title: Amendment of investigation scope for compliance
sections.summary: This amendment broadens the scope of investigations to include voluntary undertakings. The Commission may investigate organizations or individuals who fail to comply with voluntary undertakings, even after accepting them under section 48L(1).
sections.dtia_summary: This section extends the Commission's authority to investigate compliance with voluntary undertakings, impacting data transfer and processing activities through enforcement mechanisms.

requirements.text: Commission may conduct or resume investigation if organization fails to comply with voluntary undertaking

sections.source_quote: To avoid doubt, despite subsection (3)(ca), the Commission may conduct or resume an investigation under this section at any time if an organisation or a person fails to comply with a voluntary undertaking given by the organisation or person under section 48L(1) in relation to any matter.
sections.relevance_reason: investigation scope for compliance

sections.heading: Section 51 of the principal Act is amended —
sections.title: Amendment of offences for unauthorized data access
sections.summary: This amendment defines new offences for unauthorized access to personal data, including requests for data access, changes, and data porting without authorization. It also sets fines for non-compliance with reporting requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes specific offences for unauthorized data access and porting, directly impacting data transfer compliance through defined penalties and reporting obligations.

requirements.text: Person commits offence if they make unauthorized request for data access
Person commits offence if they make unauthorized request for data changes
Person commits offence if they give unauthorized data porting request
Individuals face fine up to $5,000 or imprisonment up to 6 months
Organizations face fine up to $10,000

sections.source_quote: A person shall be guilty of an offence if the person — (a) makes a request under section 21(1) to obtain access to personal data about another individual without the authority of that other individual; (b) makes a request under section 22(1) to change personal data about another individual without the authority of that other individual; or (c) subject to subsection (1A), gives a porting organisation a data porting request under section 26H(1) to transmit personal data about another individual to a receiving organisation without the authority of that other individual.
sections.relevance_reason: core offence for unauthorized data access

sections.heading: Section 52 of the principal Act is repealed and the following sections substituted
sections.title: Corporate liability for data breaches: state of mind evidence and personal liability
sections.summary: This section establishes that a corporation's state of mind in relation to an offence can be proven by evidence of an officer, employee or agent's state of mind acting within their authority. It also defines when individuals involved in a corporation's conduct are liable for the corporation's offences, including consent, connivance, or failure to prevent offences. The section specifies that individuals may rely on corporate defences and bears the same burden of proof as the corporation. It explicitly excludes the Penal Code and Evidence Act from affecting this section's application and clarifies that the corporation's liability remains unaffected by the individual's conviction.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies corporate liability for data breaches by defining when individuals are jointly liable with corporations, which impacts DTIA assessments by requiring explicit evidence of state of mind and specific conditions for individual liability.

requirements.text: Evidence that an officer, employee or agent engaged in conduct within their authority had the state of mind is evidence the corporation had that state of mind
A person who is an officer, individual involved in management, or knowingly concerned in the offence shall be liable for the corporation's offence
A person may rely on defences available to the corporation and bears the same burden of proof
This section does not affect the application of Chapters V and VA of the Penal Code
This section does not affect the corporation's liability for offences
The Minister may make regulations for application to corporations outside Singapore

sections.source_quote: Section 52 of the principal Act is repealed and the following sections substituted therefor: 'Offences by corporations'
sections.relevance_reason: Corporate liability for data breaches

sections.heading: Section 65(2) of the principal Act is amended —
sections.title: Amendment to data breach notification requirements
sections.summary: This section amends the principal Act by adding specific requirements for notifiable data breaches, including investigation steps, notification procedures to the Commission and affected individuals, and data porting requests. It mandates detailed information for porting requests, response timelines, data accessibility checks, and fee structures. The amendment also updates references to other sections to align with new provisions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section introduces specific requirements for data breach notifications and data porting, which are critical for DTIA assessments as they define the procedural safeguards and notification obligations for cross-border data transfers involving breaches.

requirements.text: Organisations must assess and notify notifiable data breaches
Organisations must provide steps for investigation and assessment of data breaches
Organisations must notify the Commission and affected individuals of notifiable data breaches
Organisations must specify the form and manner of notification for notifiable data breaches
Organisations must define information and particulars for data porting requests
Organisations must specify response timelines for data porting requests
Receiving organisations must confirm accessibility and completeness of data transmitted
Porting organisations may charge fees for data porting requests

sections.source_quote: Section 65(2) of the principal Act is amended — (a) by inserting, immediately after paragraph (b), the following paragraphs: (ba) the assessment and notification of notifiable data breaches, including — (i) the steps and measures that an organisation must take in relation to the investigation and assessment of data breaches; and (ii) the form and manner in which the Commission and affected individuals must be notified of notifiable data breaches

sections.heading: The principal Act is amended by inserting, immediately after section 68, the
sections.title: Vital interests exception for data collection without consent
sections.summary: This section establishes that personal data collection, use or disclosure is permissible without consent when necessary for an individual's vital interests, such as when consent cannot be obtained timely or the individual would not reasonably withhold consent. It requires organisations to notify individuals promptly of the collection, use or disclosure and its purpose.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides a legal exception for data collection without consent when vital interests are at stake, which impacts DTIA assessments by allowing data transfers for critical purposes without consent, subject to immediate notification.

requirements.text: Collection, use or disclosure of personal data is necessary for vital interests of the individual
Consent cannot be obtained in a timely way
The individual would not reasonably be expected to withhold consent
Organisation must notify individual of collection, use or disclosure and purpose as soon as practicable

sections.source_quote: Section 17(1) and Fifth and Twelfth Schedules: 'The collection, use or disclosure (as the case may be) of personal data about an individual is necessary for any purpose which is clearly in the interests of the individual, and — (a) consent for the collection, use or disclosure (as the case may be) cannot be obtained in a timely way; or (b) the individual would not reasonably be expected to withhold consent.'

sections.title: Excluded circumstances for porting data transmission
sections.summary: A porting organisation is exempt from transmitting applicable data under section 26H(2) in circumstances where data transmission would unreasonably interfere with operations due to repetitive requests, the burden or expense is unreasonable, the data does not exist or is trivial, or the request is frivolous or vexatious. These exclusions apply to the organisation's obligations regarding data porting requests.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines specific circumstances under which data transmission is not required, which is critical for DTIA assessments to determine when data porting obligations are waived without affecting cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: A porting organisation is not required to transmit any applicable data under section 26H(2) in any of the following circumstances
transmitting the applicable data will unreasonably interfere with the operations of the porting organisation because of the repetitious or systematic nature of the data porting request
the burden or expense of transmitting the applicable data is unreasonable to the porting organisation or disproportionate to the individual's interests
the data porting request relates to applicable data that — (i) does not exist or cannot be found; or (ii) is trivial
the data porting request is frivolous or vexatious

sections.source_quote: A porting organisation is not required to transmit any applicable data under section 26H(2) in any of the following circumstances: (a) transmitting the applicable data will unreasonably interfere with the operations of the porting organisation because of the repetitious or systematic nature of the data porting request; (b) the burden or expense of transmitting the applicable data is unreasonable to the porting organisation or disproportionate to the individual's interests; (c) the data porting request relates to applicable data that — (i) does not exist or cannot be found; or (ii) is trivial; (d) the data porting request is frivolous or vexatious.

sections.heading: In this Part —
sections.title: Definitions for broadcasting services and news organizations
sections.summary: This section defines key terms including 'broadcasting service', 'news activity', 'news organisation', 'newspaper', and 'relevant broadcasting service'. These definitions are critical for understanding the scope of broadcasting and news-related activities under Singapore's legal framework, particularly for entities operating in the media sector.

requirements.text: broadcasting service has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Broadcasting Act (Cap. 28)
news activity means the gathering of news or preparation of articles for dissemination to the public
news activity means the dissemination of news, observations on news, or current affairs to the public
news organisation means any organisation whose business consists of news activity
news organisation means any organisation that provides a broadcasting service in Singapore with a licence
newspaper has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Newspaper and Printing Presses Act
relevant broadcasting service means free-to-air nationwide television services
relevant broadcasting service means free-to-air localised television services
relevant broadcasting service means free-to-air international television services
relevant broadcasting service means subscription nationwide television services
relevant broadcasting service means subscription localised television services
relevant broadcasting service means subscription international television services
relevant broadcasting service means special interest television services
relevant broadcasting service means free-to-air nationwide radio services
relevant broadcasting service means free-to-air localised radio services
relevant broadcasting service means free-to-air international radio services
relevant broadcasting service means subscription nationwide radio services
relevant broadcasting service means subscription localised radio services
relevant broadcasting service means subscription international radio services
relevant broadcasting service means special interest radio services

sections.source_quote: 6. In this Part — "broadcasting service" has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Broadcasting Act (Cap. 28); "news activity" means — (a) the gathering of news, or the preparation or compilation of articles or programmes of or concerning news, observations on news, or current affairs, for the purposes of dissemination to the public or any section of the public; or (b) the dissemination, to the public or any section of the public, of any article or programme of or concerning — (i) news; (ii) observations on news; or (iii) current affairs; "news organisation" means — (a) any organisation — (i) the business of which consists, in whole or in part, of news activity carried out in relation to a relevant broadcasting service, a newswire service or the publication of a newspaper; and (ii) which, if the organisation publishes a newspaper in Singapore which is not exempted from the provisions of Part III of the Newspaper and Printing Presses Act (Cap. 206), is a newspaper company defined in section 2(1) of that Act; or (b) any organisation which provides a broadcasting service in or from Singapore and holds a broadcasting licence granted under section 8 of the Broadcasting Act; "newspaper" has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Newspaper and Printing Presses Act; "relevant broadcasting service" means any of the following licensable broadcasting services within the meaning of the Broadcasting Act: (a) free-to-air nationwide television services; (b) free-to-air localised television services; (c) free-to-air international television services; (d) subscription nationwide television services; (e) subscription localised television services; (f) subscription international television services; (g) special interest television services; (h) free-to-air nationwide radio services; (i) free-to-air localised radio services; (j) free-to-air international radio services; (k) subscription nationwide radio services; (l) subscription localised radio services; (m) subscription international radio services; (n) special interest radio services.
sections.relevance_reason: Definitions for media-specific terms

sections.heading: The personal data about an individual —
sections.title: Personal data provided by individual for service purposes
sections.summary: This section defines personal data provided by an individual to an organisation for the individual's personal or domestic purposes. The data must be collected, used or disclosed solely for that specific purpose. The organisation cannot use the data for any other purpose without explicit consent or a separate legal basis.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines when personal data can be collected from an individual without consent for specific purposes, relevant for DTIA when assessing data collection for service provision.

requirements.text: Personal data provided by an individual to enable service for their personal or domestic purposes

sections.title: Personal data in employment documents
sections.summary: This section defines personal data included in documents produced for an individual's employment, business or profession. The data must be collected, used or disclosed solely for purposes consistent with the document's original purpose. This ensures data collected in employment contexts remains aligned with its intended use.
sections.dtia_summary: Clarifies data collection in employment contexts, relevant for DTIA when assessing data retention and use in employment-related documents.

requirements.text: Personal data included in a document produced in the course of the individual's employment, business or profession
Data collected, used or disclosed solely for purposes consistent with the document's original purpose

sections.source_quote: 9. sections.heading: The collection of personal data about an individual, if —
sections.title: Excluded circumstances for porting data transmission
sections.summary: A porting organisation is exempt from transmitting applicable data under section 26H(2) in circumstances where data transmission would unreasonably interfere with operations due to repetitive requests, the burden or expense is unreasonable, the data does not exist or is trivial, or the request is frivolous or vexatious. These exclusions apply to the organisation's obligations regarding data porting requests.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines specific circumstances under which data transmission is not required, which is critical for DTIA assessments to determine when data porting obligations are waived without affecting cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: A porting organisation is not required to transmit any applicable data under section 26H(2) in any of the following circumstances
transmitting the applicable data will unreasonably interfere with the operations of the porting organisation because of the repetitious or systematic nature of the data porting request
the burden or expense of transmitting the applicable data is unreasonable to the porting organisation or disproportionate to the individual's interests
the data porting request relates to applicable data that — (i) does not exist or cannot be found; or (ii) is trivial
the data porting request is frivolous or vexatious

sections.source_quote: A porting organisation is not required to transmit any applicable data under section 26H(2) in any of the following circumstances: (a) transmitting the applicable data will unreasonably interfere with the operations of the porting organisation because of the repetitious or systematic nature of the data porting request; (b) the burden or expense of transmitting the applicable data is unreasonable to the porting organisation or disproportionate to the individual's interests; (c) the data porting request relates to applicable data that — (i) does not exist or cannot be found; or (ii) is trivial; (d) the data porting request is frivolous or vexatious.

sections.heading: The Eighth Schedule to the principal Act is amended —
sections.title: Definition of ongoing relationship for cross-border data transfers
sections.summary: This section amends the Eighth Schedule to define 'ongoing relationship' for cross-border data transfers. It specifies that an ongoing relationship must exist between sender and recipient on an ongoing basis, arising from business or activity conduct by the sender. The definition excludes messages not related to such relationships or those with other purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: Clarifies what constitutes an ongoing relationship for cross-border data transfers under the PDPA, impacting how data transfers between entities in ongoing business relationships are evaluated.

requirements.text: Ongoing relationship means a relationship on an ongoing basis between sender and recipient arising from business or activity conduct by sender

sections.source_quote: In sub-paragraph (1)(e), "ongoing relationship" means a relationship, on an ongoing basis, between the sender and the recipient of the message, arising from the carrying on or conduct of a business or an activity (commercial or otherwise) by the sender.
sections.relevance_reason: Core cross-border transfer definition

sections.title: Commission powers for investigation of data breaches
sections.summary: This section grants the Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC) authority to conduct investigations into data breaches. It allows the Commission to require written notices for information or documents relevant to investigations, compel attendance of individuals, and examine them orally under specific conditions. The examined person must state facts truthfully but may refuse to disclose information that could expose them to criminal charges.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides mechanisms for the PDPC to investigate data breaches, relevant for DTIAs when assessing breach response capabilities and third-party data handling.

requirements.text: Commission may require written notice to provide information or documents relevant to investigation within specified time and manner
Commission may require individuals within Singapore to attend before Commission or inspector
Commission may examine orally any person acquainted with facts or circumstances of matter
Person examined must state facts truthfully except where disclosure might expose to criminal charge, penalty or forfeiture
Statement must be reduced to writing, read over to person, interpreted if needed, and signed after correction

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of an investigation under section 50, the Commission or an inspector may do all or any of the following: require, by written notice, any person whom the Commission or inspector reasonably believes has any information, or any document in the person's custody or control, that is relevant to the investigation, to provide that information or produce that document, within the time and in the manner specified in the written notice;
sections.relevance_reason: breach investigation powers

sections.heading: Section 14 of the Monetary Authority of Singapore Act (Cap. 186, 1999 Ed.) is
sections.title: Repeal of section 14 of Monetary Authority Act
sections.summary: Section 14 of the Monetary Authority of Singapore Act is repealed and replaced with new sections 14A, 14B, and 14C, which define specific obligations regarding confidentiality, misuse of information, and re-identification of anonymised data. sections.heading: In sections 14A, 14B and 14C —
sections.title: Definitions for data protection sections
sections.summary: This section provides definitions for key terms used in sections 14A, 14B, and 14C, including 'gain', 'generally available information', 'harm', 'loss', 'personal data', and 'prescribed circumstances' or 'prescribed purpose'. These definitions clarify the scope of obligations for handling information within the Monetary Authority.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions are relevant for DTIA as they specify what constitutes personal data and other critical terms that impact data handling and transfer mechanisms.

requirements.text: gain means — (a) a gain in property or a supply of services, whether temporary or permanent; or (b) an opportunity to earn remuneration or greater remuneration or to gain a financial advantage otherwise than by way of remuneration

generally available information means information that consists of readily observable matter, including information that consists of deductions, conclusions or inferences made or drawn from readily observable matter
harm, in relation to an individual, means — (a) any physical harm; or (b) harassment, alarm or distress caused to the individual

loss means — (a) a loss in property or a supply of services, whether temporary or permanent; or (b) a loss of an opportunity to earn remuneration or greater remuneration or to gain a financial advantage otherwise than by way of remuneration, but excludes, in relation to an individual, the loss of personal data about the individual
personal data has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (Act 26 of 2012)
prescribed circumstances or prescribed purpose means any circumstances or purpose prescribed in regulations made under section 14D

sections.heading: The Spam Control Act (Cap. 311A, 2008 Ed.) is amended —
sections.title: Amendments to Spam Control Act
sections.summary: The Spam Control Act is amended to include instant messaging accounts as valid electronic addresses, define instant messaging services, and adjust the scope of the Act to exclude unsolicited commercial messages sent to instant messaging accounts. These changes impact how electronic communications are regulated.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is irrelevant to DTIA as it pertains to spam regulations and does not address cross-border data transfers or international data flows.
sections.source_quote: The Spam Control Act (Cap. 311A, 2008 Ed.) is amended — (a) by inserting, immediately after the words "electronic mail address" in the definition of "electronic address" in section 2, the words ", an instant messaging account"; (b) by inserting, immediately after the definition of "electronic address" in section 2, the following definitions: "instant messaging account" means an account of a user of an instant messaging service; "instant messaging service" means a messaging service that allows a user to exchange messages with other users who are using the service concurrently; (c) by inserting, immediately after section 4, the following section: "Electronic messages sent to instant messaging accounts"; (d) by deleting sub-paragraph (ii) of section 7(2)(b) and substituting the following sub-paragraph: (ii) an entity — (A) which is formed or recognised under the law of Singapore; or (B) which has an office or a place of business in Singapore; (e) by repealing section 8 and substituting the following section: "Application of this Part"; (f) by deleting the word "Every" in paragraph 3(1) of the Second Schedule and substituting the words "Subject to sub-paragraph (3), every"; and (g) by inserting, immediately after sub-paragraph (2) of paragraph 3 of the Second Schedule, the following sub-paragraph: (3) Sub-paragraph (1) does not apply to any unsolicited commercial electronic message that is sent to an instant messaging account.

sections.heading: The Sixth Schedule to the Supreme Court of Judicature Act (Cap. 322, 2007 Ed.),
sections.title: Amendment to Public Sector Governance Act for public sector data use defences
sections.summary: This section amends the Public Sector (Governance) Act 2018 to define specific defences for individuals in public sector data misuse cases. It establishes conditions under which a defendant can claim immunity from prosecution, including proving that information was generally available, disclosed legally, or authorized by court order. The amendments also introduce precise definitions for 'harm', 'loss', and 'personal data' to clarify legal standards in public sector data handling.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies defences for public sector data misuse but has limited direct relevance to cross-border data transfers as it focuses on internal public sector agency data handling rather than international data flows.

requirements.text: In proceedings for an offence under subsection (1), it is a defence for the defendant to prove, on a balance of probabilities, any of the following: the information under the control of the Singapore public sector agency was, at the time of its disclosure by the defendant, generally available information

In proceedings for an offence under subsection (1), it is a defence for the defendant to prove, on a balance of probabilities, any of the following: the defendant disclosed or caused the disclosure of information under the control of a Singapore public sector agency as permitted or required by or under an Act or other law (apart from this Act)

In proceedings for an offence under subsection (1), it is a defence for the defendant to prove, on a balance of probabilities, any of the following: the defendant disclosed or caused the disclosure of information under the control of a Singapore public sector agency as authorised or required by an order of court

In proceedings for an offence under subsection (1), it is a defence for the defendant to prove, on a balance of probabilities, any of the following: the defendant disclosed or caused the disclosure of information under the control of a Singapore public sector agency in any other circumstances, or for any other purpose, prescribed

If an individual makes use of information under the control of a Singapore public sector agency (A), the use is not authorised by any data sharing direction given to A, the individual is a relevant public official of A or another Singapore public sector agency at the time of the use, and the individual does so knowing that the use is not in accordance with such a data sharing direction or recklessly as to whether the use is in accordance with such a data sharing direction

If an individual makes use of information under the control of a Singapore public sector agency (A), the use is not authorised by A, the individual is a contractor supplying goods or services to A or to another Singapore public sector agency or an employee of a person who is a contractor supplying goods or services to A or to another Singapore public sector agency, and the individual does so knowing that the use is not authorised by A or recklessly as to whether the use is authorised by A

If an individual makes use of information under the control of a Singapore public sector agency (A), the use is not authorised by A, the individual is a contractor supplying goods or services to A or to another Singapore public sector agency or an employee of a person who is a contractor supplying goods or services to A or to another Singapore public sector agency, and the individual does so knowing that the use is not authorised by A or recklessly as to whether the use is authorised by A
The individual shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding $5,000 or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 2 years or to both

The definition of 'harm' means any physical harm or harassment, alarm or distress caused to the individual

The definition of 'loss' means a loss in property or a supply of services, whether temporary or permanent, or a loss of an opportunity to earn remuneration or greater remuneration or to gain a financial advantage otherwise than by way of remuneration, but excludes the loss of personal data about the individual
The definition of 'personal data' has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Personal Data Protection Act 2012
